subtitle: Microbes Are a Menace!
summary: After saving the silkworm industry, the forty-five-year-old Pasteur boldly declared that man could wipe parasitic diseases from the earth. Driven by patriotic fervor after the Franco-Prussian War, he threw himself into improving French beer, visiting breweries and studying yeast under the microscope, but discovered he loathed the taste of beer. He then turned to a heated public argument about whether yeasts arise spontaneously inside grapes, and through a series of ingenious experiments with swan-neck flasks and sterilized tubes, proved conclusively that yeasts come only from the outside surface of grapes, not from within.

visual.scene_description: Pasteur, emboldened after his silkworm success, stands amid the turmoil of the Franco-Prussian War era and channels his patriotic anger into a mission to improve French beer.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, a determined middle-aged man with neat trimmed beard and round spectacles wearing a formal black frock coat, standing in a 19th-century French study with maps and newspapers scattered on a wooden desk, one fist raised defiantly, a foaming beer stein and glass laboratory flasks visible on the table behind him, winter light through tall arched windows

segments.anchor_sentence: He embarked on vast voyages to the great breweries of France and here he questioned everybody from the brewmaster in his studio to the lowest workman that cleaned out the vats.

visual.scene_description: Pasteur visits a large brewery and peers through his microscope at yeast from beer vats, but grimaces at the taste of the beer he must sample.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, a man with neat trimmed beard and round spectacles in a black frock coat, hunched over a brass compound microscope in a brewery with large copper vats and burnished kettles, a foamy mug of beer beside him untouched, his nose wrinkled in distaste, circular inset showing round budding yeast cells with visible cell walls

segments.anchor_sentence: Pasteur did help the French beer industry.

visual.scene_description: Pasteur stands before the French Academy of Sciences, arguing passionately against Fremy's claim that yeasts generate spontaneously inside grapes.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, a man with neat trimmed beard round spectacles and formal black frock coat gesturing dramatically at a podium before rows of seated academics in a grand 19th-century French lecture hall with tall columns and arched ceiling, bunches of grapes and a swan-neck glass flask on the demonstration table beside him

segments.anchor_sentence: "So Frémy says that yeasts rise by themselves inside the grape!" cried Pasteur.

visual.scene_description: Pasteur performs his decisive experiment, using a sterilized hollow tube to extract juice from inside a grape and transferring it to a swan-neck flask, proving no yeasts exist inside the fruit.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, a man with neat trimmed beard round spectacles and black frock coat carefully piercing a ripe grape with a thin sealed glass tube at a wooden laboratory bench, rows of swan-neck flasks filled with grape juice lined up beside him, some flasks showing pink foam and others clear and still, an oil lamp illuminating the scene
